title: Hieracium subfasciculare
taxonomy: Hieracium subfasciculare is a species of flowering plant in the dandelion tribe within the sunflower family, Asteraceae. It belongs to the genus Hieracium, which is commonly known as hawkweed. This genus is further divided into several subgenera, and Hieracium subfasciculare falls under one of these subgroups. The classification of Hieracium subfasciculare is often used to study the evolution and diversity of hawkweeds. As a member of the Asteraceae family, Hieracium subfasciculare shares characteristics with other dandelion tribe members.
uses: Hieracium subfasciculare is a lesser-known species, but it has been studied for its potential medicinal properties. Its leaves and stems contain flavonoids and other compounds that may have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects. These properties could be beneficial in treating various health conditions, such as arthritis and other inflammatory diseases. However, more research is needed to fully understand the medicinal uses and potential benefits of Hieracium subfasciculare. Further studies may provide valuable insights into its applications in traditional medicine.
properties: Hieracium subfasciculare, a lesser-known species of the hawkweed genus, has been studied for its potential therapeutic properties. The plant contains flavonoids, which have been found to possess antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. Research suggests that Hieracium subfasciculare may have antimicrobial effects, making it a possible candidate for treating infections. Additionally, some studies propose that the plant may have anti-cancer properties, although further research is needed to confirm this. More studies are required to fully understand the therapeutic potential of Hieracium subfasciculare.
constituents: Hieracium subfasciculare contains a range of active constituents, including sesquiterpene lactones, flavonoids, and phenolic acids. These compounds are known to contribute to its medicinal properties. However, more research is needed to fully understand the specific roles of each constituent in the plant's effects. The sesquiterpene lactones are thought to be responsible for its anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activities. Further studies are necessary to confirm the exact mechanisms of its active constituents.

side_effects: Hieracium subfasciculare, commonly known as a species of hawkweed, is likely to be toxic if ingested due to its presence of sesquiterpene lactones and other compounds. These compounds may cause gastrointestinal issues, such as nausea and vomiting, if accidentally ingested. The plant's toxicity can also cause skin irritation in some individuals, leading to redness and itching. However, there is limited information available on the specific side effects of Hieracium subfasciculare, and more research is needed to fully understand its potential risks. If handled improperly, Hieracium subfasciculare may also cause eye irritation and allergic reactions in some people.
intro: Hieracium subfasciculare, also known as rough hawkweed, is a perennial plant species in the dandelion family (Asteraceae) characterized by its yellow flowers and hairy, lobed leaves. Traditionally, Hieracium subfasciculare has been used to treat a variety of health issues, including inflammation, digestive problems, and skin conditions, due to its purported anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. The plant contains bioactive constituents such as sesquiterpene lactones (e.g., 11,13-dihydrohelenin), flavonoids (e.g., kaempferol), and phenolic acids, which are thought to be responsible for its medicinal effects. Hieracium subfasciculare is often prepared as a tincture, infusion, or tea, and is also used in salves and creams for topical application, although its use should be approached with caution due to potential toxicity and interactions with certain medications.
